summary: Fire‑hot Truckin’>Bird Song sequence and standout organ work
blurb: The show features a rare Truckin' → Bird Song → Never Trust A Woman segue that ignites the second set, plus a powerful Morning Dew and an engaging Estimated Prophet. Brent’s Hammond organ shines on “Never Trust A Woman,” while Jerry delivers occasional peaks amid uneven moments.
review: On October 17, 1981, the Dead took the stage at Paris’ Hippodrome delivering one of their more adventurous setlists of the era. The second set is anchored by a scorching Truckin' → Bird Song → Never Trust A Woman transition that fans still talk about for its rarity and fire‑hot energy, highlighted further by Brent Mydland’s soaring Hammond organ on “Never Trust A Woman.” While Jerry Garcia slipped in and out of focus—showing flashes of his usual brilliance but also the haze of a heavy night—Bob Weir stepped up with strong solo runs that helped keep the momentum alive. Phil Lesh’s bass was occasionally buried, especially on the monitor‑mix recording, yet his foundational lines steadied the band when the chemistry wavered. The show also offers memorable moments like an impassioned Morning Dew and a crowd‑rousing Estimated Prophet, capped off by a bluesy Little Red Rooster that adds nostalgic flavor to the evening.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: One recording is strongly positive, praising the unique Truckin'‑>Bird Song‑>Never Trust A Woman sequence and crystal‑clear mix. Two other recordings are mixed, noting uneven Jerry playing and thin Phil bass but still highlighting standout moments like Morning Dew and Estimated Prophet. The overall sentiment leans slightly positive due to consistent praise of key songs and performance highlights.

best_recording.reason: Crystal‑clear Charlie Miller soundboard mix; excellent balance, smooth fades, and prominent organ on “Never Trust A Woman” make it the most listenable version.

key_highlights: Rare Truckin' → Bird Song → Never Trust A Woman segue that ignites the second set.
Brent Mydland’s Hammond organ shines on “Never Trust A Woman.”
Estimated Prophet and Little Red Rooster generate strong audience interaction.
Morning Dew delivers an emotional peak despite overall mixed chemistry.

band_performance.Jerry: Uneven performance – moments of fiery guitar and vocal peaks mixed with hazy, less focused passages.
band_performance.Phil: Bass often buried in the mix but his solid foundation helped keep the band together when chemistry wavered.
band_performance.Bob: Stepped up with strong solo runs, providing much‑needed drive throughout the set.
band_performance.Brent: Organ work on “Never Trust A Woman” is a standout, though overall playing was occasionally sluggish.
